title: A comparison of different master equations for driven-dissipative dynamics in composite quantum systems: Dispersive readout in structured electromagnetic environments
authors: Prakritish Gogoi, Angela Riva, Émile Cochin, Alex Chin
abstract: Driven-dissipative qubit-resonator dynamics, which are the basis of most dispersive superconducting qubit measurement schemes, are often modeled with Lindblad master equations built from subsystem local jump operators, even when the qubit and resonator are appreciably hybridized. In this work we revisit this setting using a microscopic Bloch-Redfield approach, where dissipation is constructed in the eigenbasis of the coupled qubit-resonator Hamiltonian with a complete, frequency dependent, open system description of the transmission line environment. Here, we show that the Lindblad and Bloch-Redfield decay rates can be quantitatively different in the absence of driving, while in the driven case we demonstrate that the time-independent Redfield dissipator and its time-dependent generalization can show qualitatively different behaviors as a function of driving strength. Finally, we investigate the effects of driving in structured spectral densities, recovering the suppression of measurement-induced relaxation in the presence of a so called Purcell filter.
summary: This paper rigorously compares master equation formalisms (Lindblad vs. Bloch-Redfield) for describing open quantum systems like superconducting qubits coupled to structured environments. It demonstrates that the Bloch-Redfield approach is necessary to accurately capture frequency-dependent dissipation, correctly predicting phenomena like measurement-induced relaxation suppression via Purcell filtering, especially under strong driving fields.
main_problem: The paper compares different theoretical frameworks—Lindblad vs. Bloch-Redfield master equations—for accurately modeling driven-dissipative dynamics in composite quantum systems, especially when the environment is structured.
main_result: The Bloch-Redfield approach is shown to be superior to the standard Lindblad model because it correctly incorporates the frequency dependence of the environment, allowing for the quantitative recovery of effects like Purcell filtering.
method: The authors employ a microscopic Bloch-Redfield approach, constructing the dissipator in the eigenbasis of the coupled system, and compare its results against the standard Lindblad master equation.
model_or_system: The system consists of a qubit coupled to a resonator, which is itself coupled to a structured electromagnetic environment (transmission line). The dynamics are governed by a total Hamiltonian and analyzed using various master equations.
key_observables: Relaxation rates ($\Gamma$), cavity photon number ($\langle \hat{a}^\dagger \hat{a} angle$), and the expectation value of the qubit Pauli operator ($\langle \hat{\sigma}_z angle$).
important_parameters: Qubit frequency ($\omega_q$), resonator frequency ($\omega_r$), coupling strength ($g$), and the spectral density function $J(\omega)$ characterizing the environment structure.
main_assumptions: The analysis compares results derived under Born-Markov approximations, noting that the Rotating Wave Approximation (RWA) for the drive Hamiltonian can lead to unphysical results, necessitating the use of the full cosine drive.
figures_summary: Figures compare the ratio of relaxation rates ($\Gamma_{ ar{n}1}/\Gamma_P$) derived from different master equations (TDBR vs. Lindblad) under both RWA and full cosine driving, demonstrating the filter effect.
paper_structure: The paper systematically compares the Lindblad and Bloch-Redfield formalisms, first showing quantitative differences in decay rates, then investigating the impact of driving, and finally demonstrating the recovery of structured environment effects (Purcell filtering) using the TDBR formalism.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for quantum optics and open quantum systems, as it provides necessary theoretical rigor for modeling superconducting qubits and cavity QED systems where the environment structure (e.g., Purcell filters) dictates measurement fidelity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematic of the dispersive readout circuit, also showing the spectral density J(ω) of the transmission-line environment with the typical frequency scales of the qubit (ωq), cavity (ωr) and environmental cut-off frequency (ωc) indicated by the vertical lines.

FIG. 2: A figure to illustrate the effect of the secular approximation using a cavity driven on resonance as an example. The plots show photon number of the cavity as a function of the dimensionless parameter κt where κ/2π = 0.5 GHz is the single photon loss rate. The Bloch-Redfield results are benchmarked with the TTI-PT (Time Translation Invariant-Process Tensor) method [25], a non-perturbative technique that is numerically exact.

FIG. 3: (a): Purcell rate ΓP vs. κ behavior for the Bloch-Redfield simulations for the range κ ∈[0.05, 2.0]. The spectral density is considered to be flat in this case such that J(ω) = J(ωr) = κ. The dashed line is for the rates calculated from the expression (17). (b): ⟨ˆσz⟩ dynamics with increasing κ and the dotted line shows the data fits used to calculate the Purcell rates. (c): Bloch-Redfield vs. Lindblad simulations. The black line represents the Lindblad, the solid blue line is for the Bloch-Redfield with a flat spectral density and the dashed blue line shows Bloch-Redfield rates with an Ohmic spectral density (6). The inset is the ratio ΓBR P /ΓL P calculated from the simulations as...

FIG. 5: (a): Comparison of the Γ¯n 1 versus ¯n trend calculated from the numerical simulations and analytical expressions for κ = 1.0. The solid purple line shows the rates calculated from (21). (b): Similar comparison for κ = 0.1.

FIG. 4: (a): Relaxation rate Γ¯n 1 normalized by the Purcell rate ΓP for the respective methods as a function of mean photon number ¯n in the cavity for κ = 1.0. Here the dotted purple curve show the Γ1(¯n) versus ¯n behavior for the time-dependent Bloch-Redfield (TDBR) with an RWA drive of the form (19). The TDBR* shows the same behavior for a cosine drive (20). The ‘Static’ curve is for the Bloch-Redfield model with a static dissipator. (b): Γ¯n 1 versus ¯n for κ = 0.1.
